data.title: Edgewise Therapeutics: Despite AF Safety Issues, EDG-7500 Advancement Presses On
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Edgewise Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:EWTX) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Edgewise: Remains A Buy After Latest Becker Muscular Dystrophy Data." In the article, I noted that the company had achieved positive results from its phase 2 CANYON study, which used its drug sevasemten for the treatment of patients with Becker Muscular Dystrophy [BMD]. This program is coming along well, especially since the company accomplished another milestone relating to this specific program, which was that it completed enrollment in the global pivotal phase 2 GRAND CANYON study using this drug to treat these patients.

Data for this trial is not expected to be released until Q4 of 2026, which means that this catalyst is far off in the future. On the flip side, this doesn't mean that there isn't a milestone for investors to look forward to in the coming months.

In essence, the goal of Edgewise is to meet with the FDA during the 1st half of 2025 to discuss possible strategies for being able to file a marketing application of sevasemten for the treatment of patients with BMD. This time around, I want to focus on the release of initial data from the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study, which uses another drug known as EDG-7500 for the treatment of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [HCM]. The data was good, but it appears as though investors might have been spooked by 2 patients who were given this drug and experienced atrial fibrillation [AF].

As I will explain below, this might be rectified. However, it is hard to entirely attribute the fall in the stock price to only these serious adverse events [SAEs]. In a concurrent way, the company also enacted an underwritten offering of common stock to raise approximately $200 million. However, despite this, the company will not be hindered by this at all. Matter of fact, the cash raised will be used to conclude and report data from Part D of the CIRRUS-HM study. Not only that, but to also initiate a phase 3 study for this very same program targeting HCM [both obstructive and non-obstructive disease] in the 1st half of 2026.

EDG-7500 For The Treatment Of Patients With Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

As I noted in the beginning above, Edgewise Therapeutics is already making substantial progress in developing the use of sevasemten for the treatment of patients with Becker Muscular Dystrophy [BMD]. Discussion strategies with the FDA might end up allowing for an Accelerated Approval pathway, but this remains to be seen. Regardless, this main clinical program in the company's pipeline remains on a good trajectory nonetheless.

This time around I wanted to highlight the development of sarcomere modulator EDG-7500 for the treatment of patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy. The use of this drug is being explored in the ongoing phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM trial. Positive 4-week data was just reported from this mid-stage trial. Before going over the release of such initial data on this drug being used to treat these patients, plus any other catalysts to be released from this very program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy [HCM] is a type of disorder characterized by the thickening of the heart muscle wall. In essence, the wall of the heart muscle becomes hypertrophied. Why is this a bad thing? It is because then the heart cannot pump enough blood through because of this thickening. Besides this problem, such an issue could also result in a person experiencing an irregular heartbeat or even death. In terms of the last part, this is a major concern for HCM, in that someone could have it and not even realize it. The reason this phenomenon happens is that there are sometimes no symptoms at all. It is important to note that there are two types of HCM, and they are as follows:

  • Obstructive HCM.
  • Non-Obstructive HCM.

I bring up the fact that there are two types of HCM because the company is evaluating the use of EDG-7500 in the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study for both obstructive and non-obstructive HCM. The global hypertrophic cardiomyopathy market is expected to reach $1.35 billion by 2031. The hope is that the company is going to continue to achieve success as it relates to the advancement of this program. As I will explain directly below, the company only reached the hurdle of releasing 4-week data from the CIRRUS-HCM study. The next set of data will be a little bit of a longer period [12 weeks]. Plus, an extension period for even further long-term testing effect of EDG-7500.

In order to see whether or not EDG-7500 as a sarcomere modulator will be effective in being able to treat these patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy [HCM], the company is in the process of running the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study. This mid-stage trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 70 patients. There are two important points to make right out the gate regarding the development of this program. The first of which is that the initial data released only included 4 weeks of treatment of the drug. Secondly, this trial itself was split specifically into two parts in relation to the types of HCM patients had.

This split goes back to the two types of HCM I listed above, which are Obstructive HCM and Non-Obstructive HCM. This is a better breakdown in terms of the trial to see:

  • Part B - Safety and tolerability of multi-doses of EDG-7500 for the treatment of patients with Obstructive Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy.
  • Part C - Safety and tolerability of multi-doses of EDG-7500 for the treatment of patients with Non-Obstructive HCM.

In terms of Part B, patients who took this drug over a 4-week period saw some strong reductions in something known as the Left Ventricular Tract Outflow gradient [LVOT-G]. This measure was done both at "rest" and "post-valsalva." With that said, the reduction of both of these LVOT-G measures from baseline for those who took 100 mg of EDG-7500 were 71% and 58% respectively. In terms of Non-Obstructive HCM patients, it was noted that they had achieved a mean reduction of 42% of the biomarker NT-proBNP. If you think about it, this is actually very good. Why is that? Well, consider that the higher this biomarker is, the greater the chance someone will be at risk for heart failure. This biomarker number increases or remains high because the heart is having to work harder in terms of pumping blood generates increased levels of NT-proBNP. The point here is that the end goal is to reduce this as much as possible and avoid heart failure altogether. For the most part, this drug was safe for a majority of participants. That's because no change was observed in terms of left ventricular ejection fraction [LVEF], especially with no patient having a value below 50%. Now, on to the downside, which is likely partially to blame for today's downward stock price movement. Two patients experienced serious Adverse Events [SAEs] of atrial fibrillation [AF]. Both of such patients required cardioversion to restore them back to normal [Cardioversion in essence is sending low-energy shocks to restore regular heart rhythm.

While a patient experiencing a SAE is never a good thing, there are three items to note of importance here The first item being that such SAEs are not likely to have an impact on further testing. In essence, these two patients having AF won't likely mean the trial being halted or paused in my opinion. Secondly, the number of AFs observed in this trial is pretty much in line with what has been seen in other HCM trials that deploy a myosin inhibitor. Lastly, the company is not going to stick with this particular dosing. It has plans to optimize its dosing strategy for Part D of this phase 2 open-label CIRRUS-HCM study.

Even better than this, investors aren't going to have to wait an extended period to see further data from this mid-stage trial. What makes me believe this to be the case? It is because it believes that it should be able to release initial data from this mid-stage study, using EDG-7500 to treat both Obstructive and Non-Obstructive HCM patients, in the 2nd half of 2025. It has the intention of being in a position to initiate a phase 3 study for this program in targeting these HCM patients in the 1st half of 2026 as well.

The point here is that the short-term downtrend today, while not a good thing, is only temporary on the back of an immediate reaction. Had the company chosen to pause enrollment after a major safety issue or the FDA placed a hold, then I believe that investors should have been worried.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Edgewise Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $470.2 million as of December 31st, 2024. The company didn't have a lot of cash on hand, and this was first stated in the 10-K SEC Filing, where it believed that it would only have enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months for starters.

Secondly, it didn't waste any time raising cash either. As I noted at the beginning of this article, along with the release of data from the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study, it also enacted a public offering of common stock. That is, it announced the pricing of a $200 million underwritten offering of common stock.

The net proceeds from this offering are to be used to advance the potential commercial launch of sevasemten for the treatment of patients with BMD, the advancement of a phase 3 trial using this very same drug to treat patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD] and lastly the phase 3 studies to be initiated of EDG-7500 for both Obstructive- and Non-Obstructive HCM. Another item to be on the watch for and monitor would be the shelf registration on Form S-3ASR filed on May 10th, 2024. This is going to allow it to deploy a host of various equity and debt offerings to raise cash. This ATM Sales agreement was made with Leerink and the company can offer and shell shares of its common stock of aggregate sales proceeds of up to $175 million from time to time.

The company burns roughly $45.6 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $36.4 million in R&D expenses and then $9.2 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Edgewise Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the recently released 4-week data from the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study, which used EDG-7500 for the treatment of patients with Obstructive/Non-Obstructive HCM. The risk here is that it is gearing up to report initial 12-week data from this mid-stage study from Cohort D in the 2nd half of 2025. There is no assurance that the data to be released from this Cohort will end up being positive. Nor, that there may not be other AF issues to arise that could have a negative impact on the stock price.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the global pivotal 18-month GRAND CANYON study, using sevasemten to treat patients with Becker Muscular Dystrophy. Data from this study is expected to be released in the 2nd half of 2026. There is no assurance that upon completion of this trial, that the primary endpoint will be met with statistical significance. Nor, that it will be good enough to allow the company to file a marketing application with the FDA to seek U.S. marketing approval for this drug for these patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of its other program which is advancing in its pipeline, which is sevasemten for the treatment of patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD]. It was able to find effective doses of this drug to treat these patients from both dose-finding FOX and LYNX studies. With that said, it is looking to initiate a phase 3 study using sevasemten to treat these DMD patients.

One risk to consider here is that the company is looking to release additional data from both FOX and LYNX in the 1st half of 2025, along with plans for this program. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that upon the release of such additional data, it will end up being viewed positively. Secondly, there is no assurance that the company will decide to continue to develop sevasemten for the treatment of these patients either.

Conclusion

Edgewise Therapeutics, Inc. is trading lower this time around since I last wrote about it. Especially, in light of today's events of both 2 safety issues of AF along with the $200 million in cash it is raising. However, the long-term thesis of it remains intact. I believe this to be the case in light that the study was not halted or paused for the time being. This doesn't mean that the FDA can't halt it later on for whatever reason it deems necessary, but for now, it is being advanced further. Plus, you also have two catalysts that remain on track for now.

The first of which is 12-week data from Cohort D of the phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study in the 2nd half of 2025 and then the initiation of the phase 3 study for this very same program in the 1st half of 2026. The BMD treatment program with sevasemten remains in a good spot because enrollment was completed in February 2025. With the continued development of EDG-7500 in the ongoing phase 2 CIRRUS-HCM study targeting patients with both obstructive and Non-Obstructive HCM, plus several catalysts approaching within a 1-year period, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: RIV: Massive, Fully Covered Distribution At A Discount
data.description:
The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (NYSE:RIV) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that income-focused investors may wish to purchase as a method of achieving a very high level of income from the assets that they already possess in their portfolios. As is the case with many closed-end funds, this one does a very good job of providing investors with a much higher level of income than any fund that tracks the major equity or fixed-income indices.

As of the time of writing, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund boasts a 13.00% yield, which, as we can see here, compares very well with the yields of any of the major domestic or global indices:

As we can see, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund currently has a yield that is more than double that of any of the indices listed above. This is something that will almost certainly appeal to most investors in need of a high level of income to support themselves. After all, a $1 million investment into a fund like the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund will generate approximately $130,000 per year in income alone. That is a pretty respectable level of income that should allow most individuals to live a comfortable lifestyle anywhere in the United States. Thus, at least in terms of yield, this fund seems to be fairly attractive.

The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund also has a yield that compares very well with that of its peers. We can see that quite clearly here:

As clearly shown, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has a yield that is significantly higher than other domestic-focused hybrid closed-end funds. However, it is worth noting that hybrid funds can invest in a variety of things, including equities, fixed-income securities, convertible securities, and other things. Most of these funds focus on one or two specific classes, so not all of them truly use identical or even similar strategies. The fund shown above that has a strategy that is most comparable to that of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is the Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund. As we can see, that fund’s yield is substantially lower than that of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund.

There are a few other funds that arguably invest in similar assets, such as the Saba Capital & Income Fund (BRW) and the High Income Securities Fund (PCF). Those two funds have much higher yields than the ones shown in the chart above:

Fund Name

Current Yield

Saba Capital & Income Fund

13.13%

High Income Securities Fund

10.75%

The yields of these two funds are much closer to that of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund. This is almost certainly because these funds have some similarities in terms of the assets in which they invest (namely, the securities in the portfolios of all three funds tend to have very high yields), but neither one of them is a perfect peer in terms of the assets that they hold either. The Saba Capital & Income Fund also has a somewhat activist strategy that might not be to everybody’s taste, so that is another way in which it is not a perfect peer.

In any case, we can see that the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is likely to appeal to anyone who needs to earn a very high level of income from the assets that they possess. This has been a challenge over most of the past decade, as bond yields have been too low to earn a respectable income, stock yields have likewise been too low with the yield of the S&P 500 Index (SP500) rarely exceeding 2%, and bank savings accounts and certificates of deposit certainly did not offer an attractive yield. Bond yields are somewhat higher today, but they are still too low to provide retirees with anything approaching a respectable income. For example, as of today, the ten-year U.S. Treasury note only has a 4.13% yield:

That works out to $41,300 of annual income from a $1 million investment. While that might be enough to support an individual or married couple in retirement, depending on their Social Security and pension benefits, it is probably not enough to truly satisfy anyone who managed to save up a million dollars over their lifetime.

Plus, bonds also have the issue of having failed as a store of value over the past decade. I explained this in detail in two recent articles (see here and here). There is no real reason to repeat this analysis in this article, but suffice it to say that U.S. government bonds have delivered a negative real total return over the past decade. This alone should dissuade anyone from putting too much faith in U.S. Treasury securities right now, regardless of their yields. A 4.13% yield on bonds is not particularly attractive if the investor’s purchasing power declines over the lifetime of the bond. As such, income investors should stick to things that deliver a positive real return, as this helps maintain the purchasing power of the principal while still providing an attractive yield. The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has a high enough yield to accomplish this task. Indeed, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has managed to deliver a positive total real return since its inception in December 2015:

This chart shows the total real returns that investors received since the December 23, 2024, inception date of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund in both the fund and in the S&P 500 Index. The chart also assumes that investors reinvested all dividends and distributions that they received back into the same asset. It also adjusts for inflation, using the Consumer Price Index as the measure of inflation. As we can see, investors in the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund received a 48.78% total real return since this fund’s inception using those assumptions. This is not as good as investors in the S&P 500 Index received, but it is still better than the negative real return of bonds over the same period.

Recent Performance Analysis

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund in early winter of 2025. My previous article on this fund was published to Seeking Alpha on January 14, 2025, so about two-and-a-half months have passed since our last discussion. As we all know, a lot can happen in the financial markets in a short period of time, and this two-and-a-half-month period is no exception to this.

One of the most important events that has occurred since our last discussion is that President Trump began announcing the tariffs that he intends to impose on various nations going forward. This has created some uncertainty in the markets, which is largely because investors do not know exactly what impact the imposed tariffs will have on corporate profits or on the economy as a whole. As we all know, markets hate uncertainty, so this has resulted in some market volatility. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) in particular was a notable victim of this volatility, as it posted a 3.59% decline over the period:

Another reason for this decline in the S&P 500 Index is that some analysts and media pundits have been loudly voicing predictions that the United States will enter into a recession during either the second half of 2025 or sometime in 2026. In many cases, equities will decline in advance of a recession due to the fear and uncertainty surrounding such events as well as the fact that corporate profits usually decline whenever a recession occurs as American consumers cut back on spending, and consumer spending is the majority of American gross domestic product. However, as I stated in a recent article, the actual data on whether or not a near-term recession is a real possibility appears to show a mixed picture.

In short, “soft data,” which is derived from surveys and other ways of judging people’s opinions about the future, points towards a recession. However, “hard data,” which is simply the numbers that show what individuals and businesses are actually doing, remains quite strong and shows that companies are still actively engaged in expansion activities. This has led some, including myself, to declare that the recession predictions appear to be politically motivated. However, expectations about the future have a way of turning into reality, so we should not exclude them from consideration regardless of whether they are politically motivated or not.

The bond market has also reacted to the uncertainty surrounding the impact of tariffs and the predictions of a recession. In this case, though, these concerns have been beneficial for bonds. Investors frequently purchase bonds in the face of uncertainty due to the fact that bonds have historically worked well to preserve the value of principal while providing a small yield to compensate for the time being sacrificed by the individual by not spending the principal today. While they have failed as a store of value over the past ten years, there are still many individuals and institutions that will opt to purchase bonds in the face of uncertainty in order to avoid losing even more money in a volatile stock market.

Investment-grade bonds, and especially government bonds, tend to benefit from this more than junk bonds due to investment-grade bonds having a much lower probability of default. The buying pressure from investors trying to move their money into bonds that are perceived to be safe naturally raises the price of these bonds (and by extension lowers their yield). We can see this here:

As this chart shows, domestic investment-grade bonds are up a respectable 3.21% since the last time that we discussed the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund. Junk bonds, on the other hand, are flat to down slightly over the same period. Thus, investment-grade bonds have been outperforming junk bonds and have been the biggest beneficiary of the flight-to-safety trade that has gone on in the market over the past two-and-a-half months. That is exactly what we would expect to see in the early stages of a recession or in response to the market’s uncertainty about the impact of tariffs or trade wars on corporate profits and the economy.

The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is a hybrid fund, which means that it invests in both equities and fixed-income securities. As such, we would expect that the poor performance of equities over the period would have an adverse impact on the fund’s share price. The fund may have been able to offset this partially with bonds, but closed-end funds rarely invest much money in investment-grade bonds. Thus, we should probably not expect anything too amazing out of this fund over the period.

In fact, though, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund delivered a performance that was not too bad given the volatility of the markets. As we can see here, the fund’s share price has only declined by 0.83% since the last time that we discussed it:

This is, admittedly, worse than either domestic investment-grade or domestic junk bonds delivered over the period, but it was far better than the 3.59% decline of the S&P 500 Index. That is what we would expect from a hybrid fund, and truthfully, I expected it to decline much more than it did. We should have a look at the fund’s composition, since if it achieved this by increasing its bond allocation, then that shows remarkably good timing on the part of the fund’s management.

While the fund’s share price performance over the period was not at all bad, investors in this fund actually did much better than the price performance suggests. As I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund’s price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

We should therefore include the distributions that the fund paid out during the period in our analysis of its performance. When we do this, we get this alternative to the performance chart shown above:

As we can see, shareholders in the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund actually did remarkably well over the period in question. Overall, investors who purchased this fund on the date that my previous article was published are now sitting on a 2.38% total return. This was better than junk bonds over the period due to the fact that this fund has a significantly higher yield than the junk bond index. The fund also outperformed the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR), but that is not particularly surprising since the low yield of that index tends to result in the dividends not doing very much over a short-term period. Unfortunately, though, this fund did underperform the total return that was provided by investment-grade bonds over this particular period. This fund was obviously not as big a beneficiary as investment-grade bonds from the flight-to-safety trade. That is not really surprising, though.

Honestly, it is very difficult to be disappointed with this fund’s performance over the past two-and-a-half months, considering all that has been going on in the market over the same period. While the fund did underperform investment-grade bonds, investors should still be reasonably pleased with it overall.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of total return. This objective makes a great deal of sense when we consider the strategy that the fund employs in order to achieve it. The fund’s strategy is detailed in its most recent annual report:

The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objective by pursuing a tactical asset allocation strategy and opportunistically investing under normal circumstances in closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, business development companies and special purpose acquisition companies. BDCs are a type of closed-end fund that invests in small companies in the initial stages of their development and are similar to venture capital funds. SPACs are collective investment structures that pool funds in order to seek potential acquisition opportunities. The Adviser has the flexibility to change the Fund’s asset allocation based on its ongoing analysis of the equity, fixed income and alternative asset markets. The Adviser considers various quantitative and qualitative factors relating to the domestic and foreign securities markets and economies when making asset allocation and security selection decisions. While the Adviser continuously evaluates these factors, material shifts in the Fund’s asset class exposures will typically take place over longer periods of time. In addition, the Fund, in seeking to achieve its investment objective, will not take activist positions in the Underlying Funds or SPACs.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its Managed Assets in Underlying Funds and SPACs. The Fund directly, and therefore Common Stockholders indirectly, will bear the expenses of the Underlying Funds or SPACs.

Under normal market conditions: (i) no more than 80% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “equity” Underlying Funds and SPACs; (ii) no more than 60% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “fixed income” Underlying Funds and SPACs; (iii) no more than 30% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “global equity” Underlying Funds and SPACs; (iv) no more than 15% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “emerging market equity” Underlying Funds; (v) no more than 30% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “high yield” and “senior loan” Underlying Fund’s and SPACs; (vi) no more than 15% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “emerging market income” Underlying Funds and SPACs; (vii) no more than 10% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “real estate” Underlying Funds and SPACs; and (viii) no more than 15% of the Fund’s Managed Assets will be invested in “energy master limited partnership” Underlying Funds and SPACs. Underlying Funds and SPACs included in the 30% limitation applicable to investments in “global equity” Underlying Funds and SPACs may include Underlying Funds and SPACs that invest a portion of their assets in emerging markets securities. The Fund will also limit its investments in closed-end funds that have been in operation for less than one year to no more than 10% of the Fund’s Managed Assets. The Fund will not invest in inverse ETFs and leveraged ETFs. The types of Underlying Funds and SPACs referenced in this paragraph will be categorized in accordance with the Fund categories established and maintained by Morningstar, Inc.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that the Saba Capital & Income Fund is fairly well known for its activist style of investing. Basically, Saba Capital will invest money into other funds that it perceives as being undervalued relative to the value of their assets. For example, if a fund is trading at a discount due to some problem with the fund, Saba Capital might purchase shares of the fund and then use the voting power to force management to make changes that are intended to solve the problem and bring the price closer to net asset value. Bulldog Investors, which manages the Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) is known for doing the same thing.

However, the first paragraph in the quoted strategy description above implicitly states that RiverNorth does not employ similar tactics in its management of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund. Rather, this fund appears to simply be purchasing other closed-end funds and simply collecting the distributions that they pay out, as well as benefiting from capital gains. The fund’s management is not really pushing for changes in the funds in which it invests. This may be appealing to some investors, as not everybody likes activist strategies and some prefer a more “hands off” approach to their investments.

The most recent schedule of investments for the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is found in its semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024. This document provides the following asset allocation for the fund as of that date:

Please recall that the last time that we looked at this fund, the most recent schedule of investments provided the fund’s holdings as of September 30, 2024. The figure today is 80.51% of net assets, so the fund’s allocation to that asset class increased by 3.41% over the three-month period. The biggest changes here are that the fund’s allocation to closed-end fund common equities and special purpose acquisition companies increased substantially since the last time that we discussed the fund. This was partially offset by the decrease in the fund’s exposure in both its cash equivalents (money market fund) and U.S. Treasuries. The U.S. Treasury decrease appears to simply be caused by some of the fund’s U.S. Treasury obligations maturing, so it simply used the cash for something else rather than buying new Treasury securities. It is unclear what the fund did with the cash that it pulled out of the money market fund, but it was likely used to purchase additional assets.

Honestly, that is a very good move on the part of the fund since nearly all closed-end funds and business development companies have higher yields than U.S. Treasuries and money market securities. Thus, it seems certain that whatever the fund purchased with the cash has a higher yield than it was previously receiving, which boosted its income somewhat over the period.

The overwhelming majority of the fund’s closed-end fund holdings are domiciled in the United States. The semi-annual report states that 69.73% of the fund’s net assets were invested in American closed-end funds as of December 31, 2024. However, not all of them were as the report still mentions that 10.78% of the fund’s net assets were invested in a single British closed-end fund as of that date:

Pershing Square Holdings (OTCPK:PSHZF) is a name that we sometimes see among the holdings of closed-end funds that invest in other closed-end funds. The fund is based in Guernsey, but it is managed by Pershing Square Capital Management, L.P., which is an American hedge fund manager. The fund’s website offers the following description of its strategy:

Pershing Square Holdings, Ltd. is an investment holding company structured as a closed-end fund principally engaged in the business of acquiring and holding significant (but generally not controlling) positions in a concentrated number of large capitalization companies. PSH’s objective is to maximize its long-term compound annual rate of growth in intrinsic value per share.

Pershing Square Holdings is not an American closed-end fund and as such it is not subject to American regulations. The regulatory differences are one reason why the fund only has a 1.34% yield as opposed to the much higher yields of most domestic closed-end funds. The fund’s historical performance has not been bad at all, though, as it has delivered a 167.5% total return over the past ten years:

As such, the fact that the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has a large position in Pershing Square Holdings is not necessarily a hindrance to the fund’s ability to generate investment profits for its shareholders. After all, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is targeting the generation of total return and not necessarily current income from its portfolio assets.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield and total return that the fund obtains from the assets in its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund is borrowing money and using those borrowed funds to purchase shares of closed-end funds, business development companies, and similar entities. As long as the purchased assets deliver a higher yield than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio.

This fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, so this will usually be the case. It is important to note though that this strategy is not as effective today with rates at 6% as it was three years ago when rates were effectively 0%. This is because the difference between the rate that the fund has to pay and the yield that it can get on the purchased assets is much narrower than it once was.

Unfortunately, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not employing too much leverage because that would expose us to too much risk. I generally do not like a fund’s leverage to exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 34.12% of its total portfolio. This represents an increase over the 33.14% leverage that the fund had at the time of our last discussion, which is actually not really very surprising. After all, as we saw earlier in this article, the shares of this fund are down 0.83% since that previous article was published. We can usually assume that this decline in the fund’s share price means that its portfolio has also decreased in size slightly over the period. If this is the case, the fund’s outstanding borrowings would end up representing a higher proportion of its overall portfolio, all else being equal.

This chart shows the fund’s net asset value since the date that my previous article was published:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value decreased by 2.64% over the period in question. This is actually a much larger decrease than is suggested by the share price performance alone, which unfortunately suggests that this fund has also gotten more expensive since the date that my previous article was published. We will discuss that later, but for now, we can clearly see that the fund’s net asset value decreased, so now the outstanding borrowings are a larger percentage of its portfolio.

In the previous article, we saw that the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund had a much higher level of leverage than its peers at that time. Here is how it compares today:

(All figures from CEF Data.)

Several of these funds, including the Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund, which is the closest peer in terms of strategy, do not use leverage as part of their strategies. The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is obviously significantly more leveraged than any of those funds and thus could theoretically represent a higher risk in terms of the use of leverage. This conclusion is strengthened by the fact that the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund has the highest level of leverage among all of its peers that do use leverage. A risk-averse investor may be starting to get worried about the fund’s leverage ratio here, especially since it has been increasing over the past few months, but I am not too concerned about it yet. However, it may be time to start paying more attention to the fund’s leverage just in case the market keeps declining.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of total return. Most American closed-end funds primarily do this by paying a regular distribution out to their shareholders. The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is not an exception to this, as the fund currently pays a $0.1277 per share monthly ($1.5324 per share annually) distribution. This gives the fund a whopping 13.00% yield at the current share price.

Unfortunately, this fund has historically not been particularly consistent with respect to its distribution over its history. We can see that here:

In a previous article on this fund, I made the following observation:

The fact that the distribution has exhibited such considerable variation over the years is likely to be something of a turn-off to those investors that are seeking a safe and secure income to use to pay their bills or finance their lifestyles. The fact that the fund cut its distribution back at the start of 2023 is not likely to help this impression.

The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund cut its distribution again in January, although it was not a very large cut. Beginning in January 2025, the fund began paying a monthly distribution of $0.1277 per share, down from $0.1289 per share previously:

This was only a 0.93% cut so it is not particularly huge, but considering that inflation is still causing the cost of everything that we purchase to increase, distribution cuts are the last thing that we really need because they reduce our incomes. Thus, this cut will likely further reduce the attractiveness of the fund for certain investors.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is the semi-annual report for the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2024. A link to this report was provided earlier. This is a newer report than what was available to us previously, so let us have a look at it for an update on the fund’s financial condition as well as any insights that might shed some light on the rationale for the distribution cut.

For the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund received dividends of $13,336,065 and interest of $3,172,602 from the assets in its portfolio. This gives the fund a total investment income of $16,508,667 for the period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $13,594,105 available for shareholders. That was not sufficient to cover the $16,596,779 that the fund distributed over the six-month period. I will admit, though that this fund’s net investment income alone was a lot closer to the fund’s distribution than I expected would be the case.

The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund was able to make up for the difference via capital gains. Over the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the fund reported net realized gains of $6,714,446, which was completely offset by $10,154,898 in net unrealized losses. The fund’s net assets declined by $9,131,822 after accounting for all inflows and outflows over the period.

The fund fully covered its distributions during the period out of net investment income and net realized gains. While it is true that the fund’s net assets declined overall, that was only due to the net unrealized losses that the fund suffered from in the period. Net unrealized losses can be erased by a market rebound, so they are not necessarily permanent. For the most part, if a fund is fully covering its distributions out of net investment income and net realized gains then we do not need to worry too much. The rationale behind the distribution cut is not completely clear, but the fund’s management might be expecting lower returns in 2025, so it is attempting to prepare for that.

Valuation

Shares of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund are currently trading at a 2.30% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 2.15% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is only very slightly worse than its long-term average. Over the past three years, shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from a 13.43% discount to a 10.88% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 3.25% discount on net asset value, which is only slightly better than today’s price. As such, the current price does not appear to be too bad when compared to the fund’s long-term average valuation.

Here is how the fund’s current valuation compares with its peers:

As of the time of writing, all of the peer funds are trading at prices that are below the actual value of their underlying assets. Three of the peer funds are not very much lower, however, as they only have discounts in the 2% range. The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is one such fund, so it does appear to be expensive relative to its peers, but it is not excessively so as it is fairly close to its historical average and is still trading at a discount.

Expenses

CEF Data provides a 3.90% expense ratio for the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund, which is identical to what the fund had the last time that we discussed it. As was also the case previously, excluding the costs associated with the fund’s leverage drops the expense ratio down to 1.67%. There are no updates to provide in this respect.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund is an interesting closed-end fund with a strategy that is rarely seen in the sector. The fund primarily invests in other closed-end funds, business development companies, and other entities that boast very high yields. This positions the fund quite well to earn a high yield on its assets and distribute that to its shareholders.

The fund can certainly boast about its yield, as 13.00% is pretty hard to find anywhere in today’s market. The fund also fully covered it during the most recent year. When we consider that the RiverNorth Opportunities fund is currently trading at a discount, it is not hard to recommend it to anyone who is looking for a high yield and is not too afraid of the potential for more market volatility as the year progresses.

data.title: Asbury Automotive: Attractive Given Modest Tariff Exposure
data.description:
Shares of Asbury Automotive Group, Inc. (NYSE:ABG) have been essentially dead money over the past year, losing about 4%. More recently, shares have been on a steady decline, losing 25% from their highs as news on auto tariffs has been unfavorable. Given much of its earnings actually comes from maintenance activity, this could be an overreaction. I last covered Asbury in November, rating shares a “strong buy.” After rallying over 30% to my $300 price target, shares are back to where they were when I last covered them. Given the new tariff policy, now is a good time to revisit the stock. I remain bullish.

Last week, President Trump signed an executive order that will impose a 25% tariff on imported vehicles. As you can see below, the US imports about 60% of its vehicles. Given a retail price includes some costs after imports (i.e., sales commissions) and the likelihood that in this mixed consumer environment, the entire cost cannot be passed on to consumers, I expect average new car prices to rise about 5-8%, assuming these tariffs are indeed implemented for a sustained period of time.

Because new car prices are likely to rise, I do expect new car volumes to slow as worsened affordability saps consumer demand. This should have a knock-on effect on used car prices, as we saw post-COVID. With new cars less attractive, some consumers will likely swap to a used car, driving up prices there. I expect a smaller impact than in 2021-2022 given the lack of government stimulus. Still, I view a 10-15% increase in used car prices as likely.

These policy changes will have various impacts on different parts of Asbury’s business. As you can see, its portfolio is broadly similar to the national profile, as most of its luxury vehicles are also imported. The 30-35% of its domestic business should have limited tariff exposure, while it will likely face higher prices for the remainder of its business. All else equal, I would expect its new car vehicle sales to slow and it to face a somewhat tighter margin.

On used cars, I would expect a temporary benefit to profits as it sells its existing inventory at higher prices. However, in the medium term, it will also need to buy used cars wholesale at a higher price as well, so any gross margin gains are likely to be temporary. All else equal, higher car prices likely mean that consumers will try to hold their existing car for somewhat longer, which should increase the need for maintenance work, a positive for ABG’s service and parts business.

This is a key consideration because while car sales are the vast majority of its revenue, the majority of its gross profit actually comes from services, somewhat insulating the impact of tariffs. There should be minimal margin impact on domestic car sales from tariffs. If we assume a 20% impact on imported car margins, equating to 1-1.5% of the purchase price, and a 5% impact on luxury margins, given that these consumers are likely less price sensitive, I believe there would be a ~10% impact to ABG’s new vehicle gross profit.

That equates to a ~2.2% impact on total gross profit. ABG would need to see a ~5% improvement in parts & service gross profit to offset this impact. While we may not see an increase quite this large, I would expect some benefit, and so the true gross profit risk to the business is likely ~1%. While I do not agree with analysts at Morgan Stanley (MS) that ABG could be a winner from tariffs, I do see the impact as being fairly limited, certainly relative to how much the stock has declined.

This recent decline is all the more jarring given how well the company has been performing. In the company’s fourth quarter reported on January 30th, ABG reported earnings of $7.26, blowing past consensus by $1.22 as revenue grew by 18% to $4.5 billion. These strong results helped catapult Asbury shares to a new all-time high, though that feels like a lifetime ago, as tariffs and macro uncertainty have caused investors to look past recent results.

Still, the trends that have made me structurally bullish about ABG were evident. We continue to see its parts & service unit, which I view as the crown jewel, perform extremely well. Same-store parts and service revenue was up 6%; gross profit was up 11% thanks to a 26% jump in warranty activity. With improving utilization and efficiency efforts, we continue to see excellent margin accretion.

Now, total gross profit rose by 11% to $750 million, 7% below revenue growth, as margins declined by 101bps to 16.6%. This is to be expected, as we continue to see vehicle margins move to normal levels after spikes past COVID to unsustainable levels. While new vehicle units rose by 18%, gross profit was up just 1%, as it made $3,600 on a vehicle from $4,300 last year. Similarly, even as used vehicle volumes rose by 15%, gross profit fell by 2%. It made $1,500 on used vehicles from $1,700 last year.

As I have discussed before, I expect ABG is past its peak profitability, when supply shortages allowed dealerships to mark up new car prices, creating a short-term boost to profits. The question for the dealerships is what their long-term “normal” profits will look like. I last estimated ABG’s run-rate earnings as $21 vs the ~$27 I had been expecting this year. However, this quarter gave signs run-rate could be closer to $22-23 given ongoing cost efficiencies. Notably, SG&A was 62% of gross profit, a 141bp improvement from last year. Additionally, prior to tariffs, management expected new car margins to eventually settle in at $2,500-$3,000, better than the $2,000-$2,500 many expected.

Importantly, even as results normalize, ABG remains highly cash-generative with $526 million in free cash flow, down slightly from $563 million the year before. It also has tightly managed inventory, which should help it retain pricing power in the short term. Its $2 billion of inventory was up 12%, ending the quarter with 49 days of new cars and 37 days of used cars. Frankly, I hope it increased inventory more rapidly in Q1 to get more products purchased before tariffs take effect.

Now with its free cash flow, ABG has taken a different approach to capital allocation than its peers. As you can see below, it has primarily focused on buying more dealerships to consolidate and add scale rather than repurchase stock. It still did about $180 million of buybacks in 2024.

The company is not backing away from this strategy, either. In February, Asbury announced its acquisition of the Herb Chambers Companies, adding 52 franchises and $2.9 billion in revenue. It expects to close the deal in Q2 and is paying $1.34 billion, which it will finance via debt and cash on hand. This will give Asbury exposure to New England, a region it had been lacking previously.

ABG is paying about 0.46x sales for Herb Chalmers, which will grow its business about 16%. By comparison, ABG is currently trading at about 0.44x 2024 sales given its $7.5 billion enterprise value. With just modest cost synergies, this should be an accretive acquisition, especially given ABG’s history of driving more service activity at acquired dealerships.

In the short term, investors prefer the certainty of buybacks to M&A, so this announcement has likely contributed to softness in shares. Still, ABG has proven itself an adept acquirer. This deal will also increase leverage. ABG targets 2.5-3.0x leverage and was already at the midpoint of the range, meaning it will likely briefly pass 3.5x. I expect the company to direct all free cash flow to debt reduction, and it should be able to de-leverage by ~0.4-0.6x in a year, getting back to targeted levels during 2027.

After reaching ~$300 post-earnings, ABG shares had reached my target and were priced for a fairly good long-term outcome, which made them particularly vulnerable to a shift in the macro sentiment. Plus, an acquisition took significant buybacks off the table for 18-24 months, likely allowing shares to drift lower. Still, I see the tariff impact as very modest. Barring a recession (which is not my base case), I see $26-$27 in 2025 EPS, leaving shares at ~8.5x earnings.

Over the medium term, I see this as a company with $22-23 in earnings power, largely from stable parts & service activity, so I view a 10x normalized multiple as highly attractive. Given more debt in the short term and uncertain macro, even as I am lifting long-term EPS estimates by ~6%, I am maintaining a $300 fair value, as these factors are legitimate headwinds to valuation. Still, after such a sharp pullback, I reiterate ABG as a strong buy and would take advantage of the drop.

data.title: TCW Global Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The fourth quarter was characterized by a repricing of U.S. economic growth forecasts (higher) and the unwinding of Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut expectations, driven by the re-election of Donald Trump and the Republican sweep of Congress. Indeed, sectors most closely associated with exuberance such as equities and bitcoin surged in the immediate aftermath en route to new all-time highs and credit spreads reached multi-decade lows. Although the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by an additional 50 bps during the quarter, after transitioning to accommodative policy in September with a 50 basis point (bps) cut, markets again repriced the pace and magnitude of rate cuts, resulting in a swift sell off in U.S. Treasury yields. The yield curve bear steepened as longer dated yields soared anywhere from 65 to 80 bps due to expectations for pro-growth policies from the incoming Republican administration, while front-end yields increased 60 bps alongside a hawkish pivot by the Fed that resulted in 2025 rate cut expectations falling from four to two.

Similarly, developed market sovereign yields outside the U.S. increased even as central banks continued cutting rates. In Europe, the European Central Bank (ECB) also cut rates by 50 bps during the quarter amid a steady decline in inflation and growth, though uncertainty around potential inflationary effects of U.S. tariffs proposed by the incoming administration led to a repricing of interest rates across major European markets, with 10-Year yields ending the quarter 15-30 bps higher across curves. In the UK, though growth has been anemic, inflation remains stubborn due to elevated CPI services inflation. As such, the Bank of England has been slower to trim interest rates, cutting only 25 bps so far, and the outlook for future cuts was re-calibrated over the quarter leaving interest rates higher for the period. Meanwhile, the Royal Bank of New Zealand has been rapidly cutting rates since July given weak growth and employment data, with 100 bps of easing in the fourth quarter. In stark contrast, the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has yet to cut rates this cycle, despite weaker than expected growth and headline inflation, citing high underlying inflation. Bank of Japan is the only developed market central bank tightening policy, but the move higher in rates over the quarter was limited due to uncertainty about the impact of potential policy changes in the U.S. In line with the above, the U.S. dollar (USD) outperformed other global currencies in the fourth quarter with the DXY Index rising by 7.6%, while the Japanese yen (JPY) and Australian (AUD) and New Zealand dollars (NZD) were the weakest G10 currencies. Emerging market currency performance was differentiated, with countries less sensitive to a U.S. trade war, such as Turkey and India performing relatively better (though still lagging the USD), while the South Korean won underperformed more significantly, reflecting a combination of U.S. trade uncertainty, budget concerns and political dynamics. The Brazilian real also underperformed significantly as fiscal concerns grew.

Against a backdrop of higher yields, global fixed income returns were negative, with the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index down 5.1% for the quarter and 1.7% for the year. While almost anything with a duration above three years posted negative returns, most sectors tightened over the quarter and year, with the exception of U.S. agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), which were particularly sensitive to interest rate volatility.

Performance

The TCW Global Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) fell 6.49% in the final quarter of 2024, trailing the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index by 138 bps. As global sovereign yields rose across developed markets during the quarter, long duration positions in the U.S., UK, and Eurozone were a sizable headwind to relative performance, while the underweight to Japan duration contributed. An underweight to China duration also hurt relative performance, as yields declined during the quarter on monetary policy easing, while the long duration position in Brazil detracted given concerns about fiscal dynamics. The Fund’s currency positioning also detracted during the quarter, largely driven by an underweight to the USD, which rallied amid reduced fears of rapid rate cuts and expectations of higher U.S. growth under a Trump administration. Additionally, overweight positioning in the Japanese yen, Norwegian krone, Brazilian real, and the South Korean won also hurt relative performance. Meanwhile, the relative value position - underweight Euro (EUR) and overweight the British pound (GBP) contributed to relative performance. From an allocation perspective, relative returns were dampened by an ongoing underweight to developed market corporate credit that continued to trade inordinately rich in the TCW team’s view. While this underweight acted as a headwind, firm pricing provided opportunities to trim into strength and optimize existing holdings, leading to issue selection benefits within financials and industrials. Countering the corporate underweight was an overweight to CMBS and ABS, both of which outpaced Treasuries on a duration-adjusted basis during the quarter, while additional contributions came from the emphasis on single asset single borrower (SASB) CMBS deals which provided a carry advantage relative to the Index.

Outlook and Positioning

Global growth was both stronger and more divergent than expected in 2024, though we expect sub-consensus growth in 2025. In the U.S., weaker employment, lower net immigration, tariffs, and limited room for an increase in fiscal stimulus are downside risks to growth, while fiscal consolidation and U.S. tariff uncertainty could lead to a slowdown in European growth partially offset by looser monetary policy, and lower growth in China versus expectations given uncertainty regarding tariffs, more measured fiscal stimulus and ongoing property sector returns. Japan, however, should continue to see modest growth. Meanwhile, disinflation trends continue, providing cover for central banks to increasingly defend against downside growth risks. However, the distribution of outcomes for 2025 has widened post the U.S. election, presenting the potential for higher volatility. As such, the Fund maintains exposure across higher quality sovereign issuers with strong or improving macroeconomic outlooks that can weather volatility. Duration positioning favors advanced economies where real yields are attractive and where central banks are expected to ease policy significantly more than what’s priced in, particularly the U.S., New Zealand, and the UK. China and Japan remain the largest duration underweights as the Bank of Japan is expected to continue tightening policy in the form of reduced JGB purchases and further rate increases, while the duration underweight in China is due to low yields as the PBoC maintains easy monetary policy, and better valuations in other markets. As it relates to currency positioning, the Fund maintains an underweight to Euro-denominated assets given expectations for slower growth and accelerated rate cuts by the ECB, versus the British pound as the UK benefits from potential inflows and reduced political risk. Additionally, an underweight to the U.S. dollar and Chinese renminbi are offset by overweight positioning in the Norwegian krone given attractive valuations and improving trade balances, and the Japanese yen, which should benefit from continued tightening by the BoJ. Finally, the Fund is long Brazilian real given high real rates and FX undervaluation, though we are monitoring the fiscal dynamics closely.

TCW remains committed to our time-tested value-oriented philosophy, with bottom-up portfolio construction and relative value sector allocations the pillars by which we introduce carry and return potential to portfolios. This is reflected in a significant and broad underweight to corporate credit given tight credit spreads, though with targeted positions in high-conviction issuers and industries like communications and utilities, the latter offering a variety of opportunities to capitalize on the structural tailwind of power generation and demand. Additionally, opportunities exist in below investment grade developed and emerging market credit, with allocations focused on issuers with strong or improving growth fundamentals. Meanwhile, securitized products present a more compelling relative value proposition given wider yield spreads, driving overweight positions across residential MBS, CMBS, and ABS. Agency MBS remains the largest position given favorable liquidity, the government guarantee, and over 50 bps of spread premium versus U.S. investment grade credit. Though non-agency MBS does not have a government guarantee, the sector benefits from robust collateral profiles that help maximize cash flow back to bondholders, with the ongoing evolution of structures and deal types across the sector fertile ground to capture alpha potential at the issue level. Similarly, CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, facilitating bottom-up inclusion criteria. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations, with European CLOs comprising the bulk of the exposure and smaller positions in ABS backed by shipping containers, data centers, and select FFELP student loans.

The index is not available for direct investment; therefore its performance does not reflect a reduction for fees or expenses incurred in managing a portfolio. The securities in the index may be substantially different from those in the Fund. Portfolio characteristics and securities are subject to change at any time.

Investment Risks

It is important to note that the Fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. Government. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk, the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk, and price volatility risk. Funds investing in bonds can lose their value as interest rates rise and an investor can lose principal. Mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities often involve risks that are different from or more acute than risks associated with other types of debt instruments. MBS related to floating rate loans may exhibit greater price volatility than a fixed rate obligation of similar credit quality. With respect to non-agency MBS, there are no direct or indirect government or agency guarantees of payments in pools created by non-governmental issuers. Non-agency MBS are also not subject to the same underwriting requirements for the underlying mortgages that are applicable to those mortgage-related securities that have a government or government-sponsored entity guarantee. Fund share prices and returns will fluctuate with market conditions, currencies, and the economic and political climates where the investments are made. Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile. The Fund’s investments denominated in foreign currencies will decline in value if the foreign currency declines in value relative to the U.S. dollar. The Fund may use leverage to increase its net income, but these activities entail the risk that under certain market conditions the cost of leverage could exceed the return of the fund, reducing returns to shareholders. The use of leverage may cause a Fund to liquidate portfolio positions when it may not be advantageous to do so to satisfy its obligations or to meet segregation requirements. This may cause a Fund to be more volatile, which may increase the risk of investment loss. Shares of closed-end investment companies frequently trade at a discount to their net asset value, which may increase investors’ risk of loss. A closed- end fund is not continuously offered. After the initial public offering, shares are sold on the open market through a stock exchange. This risk may be greater for investors expecting to sell their shares in a relatively short period after completion of the public offering. Please see the Fund’s Prospectus for more information on these and other risks.

Glossary of Terms

ABS (Asset-Backed Securities)– A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Accommodative Monetary Policy– When a central bank (such as the Federal Reserve) attempts to expand the overall money supply to boost the economy when growth is slowing (as measured by GDP). Agency MBS– The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Basis Point (BPs)– One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bear Market– A bear market is a condition in which securities prices fall 20 percent or more from recent highs amid widespread pessimism and negative investor sentiment. Typically, bear markets are associated with declines in an overall market or index like the S&P 500, but individual securities or commodities can be considered to be in a bear market if they experience a decline of 20 percent or more over a sustained period of time - typically two months or more. Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index– Provides a broad-based measure of the global investment grade fixed-rate debt markets. The index is not available for direct investment; therefore its performance does not reflect a reduction for fees or expenses incurred in managing a portfolio. The securities in the index may be substantially different from those in the Fund. Portfolio characteristics and securities are subject to change at any time. BOJ (Bank of Japan)– Japanese central bank. Cash Flow– The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. Central Bank– A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. Collateral– Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit– A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation’s debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Credit Spreads– 1. The spread between Treasury securities and non-Treasury securities that are identical in all respects except for quality rating. 2. An options strategy where a high premium option is sold and a low premium option is bought on the same underlying security. Developed Markets– Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Disinflation– A slowing in the rate of price inflation. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union (EU) member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. Emerging Markets Credits– Emerging markets bonds (debt). Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FFELP (Federal Family Education Loan Program)– Student loans made through this program are funded by private lenders and are insured by guaranty agencies and reinsured by the federal government. Fiscal Stimulus– An increase in public spending or a reduction in the level of taxation that might be performed by a government in order to encourage and support economic growth. GBP– British pound. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. High Yield– A bond that is rated below investment grade. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Inflationary– Of, associated with, or tending to cause inflation. Investment Grade– A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody’s, Standard & Poor's and Fitch. Liquidity– The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. Macroeconomic– Relating to the branch of economics concerned with large-scale or general economic factors, such as interest rates and national productivity. MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by an accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Non-Agency MBS– Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. PBOC (People’s Bank of China)– China central bank. Securitized– When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Sovereign– A national government within a given country. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Tightening– Short for tight monetary policy. A situation in which a central bank enacts relatively high target interest rates to lower the available of credit. Effectively “tightening” the supply of credit. Underperformance– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. U.S. Treasury– The U.S. Treasury is the government department responsible for issuing all Treasury bonds, notes and bills. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve– A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity.
