data.title: PPG Industries: Further Pain Is Expected
data.description:
Introduction

Now that PPG Industries, Inc. (NYSE:PPG) has reported its full-year 2024 results, I wanted to see how it fared throughout the year and give some comments on the outlook in the short term and my worries regarding the overarching uncertainty of the global tariff war.

Briefly on Performance

The company’s top-line performance for the year dipped in Q4 due to weak demand in Europe, lower automotive OEM build rates as well as softer industrial production in the US, which was slightly offset by growth in aerospace and Latin American markets. Throughout the year, we can see that the company’s top line struggled with growth, which was further exaggerated in Q4.

Regarding the company’s efficiency and profitability, we can see that margins remained rather stable across the board with minor declines in some cases. The standout metric here is the company’s operating margins, which saw a decent improvement over the year due to better cost management and technology-driven sales, which is the company’s digital-technology subscription. The management has done a good job of managing the company’s costs, so I commend them for their efforts.

Continuing with efficiency and profitability, we can see that the company’s ROA and ROE remained somewhat stable as well, just like its bottom line, which is a good sign. These metrics are slightly above my minimums of 5% for ROA and 15% for ROE, which tells me that the management is utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder equity efficiently, thus creating value in the long run. I would like to see the metrics turn around and start going up in the future, but that remains to be seen.

Regarding the company’s competitive advantage and moat, I like to look at its return on total capital or ROTC. I like to see at least 10% ROTC, which would signal me that the company is enjoying some sort of competitive edge and has a moat. PPG is right at that minimum mark, which it only hit recently, with a nice upward trajectory, which could signal further ROTC gains in the future.

Comparing PPG’s ROTC to its peers (as selected by default by Seeking Alpha), we can see that PPG is close to the top, only behind RPM International Inc. (RPM), which means that there is room for improvement.

Now, let’s look at the company’s financial situation. PPG ended the year with $1.3B in cash and equivalents, against $4.9B in long-term debt. This looks like a decent gap between what the company has in capital and what it owes, however, how worrisome is this? The company’s interest coverage ratio stood at around 10x, which means the company’s operations easily cover the annual interest expenses on the debt outstanding. The company seems to have a decent interest rate locked in on such a high amount of debt. This means that not a lot of the capital is needed to sustain the debt on its books, and it is not a risk whatsoever. Additionally, the company’s decent cash pile as well as operating cash flow can easily support these interest expenses.

Overall, with some declines in the company’s top-line performance, we can see that the management has done a decent job of retaining the company’s profitability and efficiency, as well as improving it in some parts of operations, mainly operating margins. The company has a decent moat and competitive advantage, and even though it may look as if it has a lot of debt, it is not a risk due to the interest rate it has locked in, which is easily supported by the performance of the company.

Comments on the Outlook

So, what about the year 2025? What should we expect from the company? The management was kind enough to provide what they expect the company to do in 2025. They are expecting the company’s EPS to be in the range of $7.75 to $8.05, which is below the consensus estimates of $8.58. Naturally, the investment community didn’t like the miss and rewarded the company with a 10% dip in share price, which hasn’t recovered yet, mainly because of the overall market volatility and uncertainty of the economy. The company also expects a low-single-digit growth in organic sales, which is slightly better than what it achieved in 2024, although, that could easily change if the economy takes a turn for the worse.

The management expects a slow start to 2025, with the softness in Europe and global industrial end-use markets remaining a big hurdle. It appears that the company is at the mercy of the softer market demand. There is very little it can do to stimulate top-line growth if the demand is not there. Therefore, I would like the company to turn its focus to internal operations, something that can be changed for the better.

We saw how the company improved its operating margins throughout the year, so I would like this momentum to continue going forward. And how can this be achieved? The focus on high-margin products such as a very promising market segment of smart windows, is projected to grow at a respectable 11.5% through 2030, as well as other products that would be a lot more margin accretive in the future like the recently announced non-BPA coatings at the Paris Packaging Week. These coatings typically are higher priced as they cater to health-conscious consumers and industries that prioritize safety, which should be all industries in my opinion. These coatings also have the appeal of sustainability, which should drive demand, therefore, the more it sells at higher prices, the higher its margins will be. Additionally, being one of the main players in non-BPA coatings, which is a challenging task to achieve in terms of formulating it, the barriers to entry are high, therefore, companies like PPG and The Sherwin-Williams Company (SHW) will be dominating the market here, and with enjoy higher margins overall.

Now onto the uncertainty of the tariffs, which has been an overarching theme for the last couple of months now. The company faces quite a lot of risk, which can disrupt its supply chains, inflate costs, and dampen demand in many areas. Critical raw materials for making coatings like titanium oxide, will see their price increase because of the tariffs on Chinese goods, which could see further increases in tariffs if there is a back-and-forth between the US and China in the future. This will no doubt have a negative effect on the company’s margins in the future. The company also heavily relies on global supply chains, so disruption to the flow of products will further decrease efficiencies, which will lead the management to provide lower guidance.

The company is involved in the automotive sector, and we now know that the focus of the 25% tariffs will be on this segment specifically, which means we should expect further negative effects going forward. For example, Dodge, a brand from Stellantis N.V. (STLA) already reported a 33% drop in manufacturing starts in 2024, and I don’t think it is going to improve any time soon.

Closing Comments

Overall, I am not very confident in the immediate short-term and what kind of operational problems we are going to see from the tariff war in 2025. There are some positives regarding the internal operations of the company. However, I believe these will be outweighed by the uncertainty of the tariffs and the ripple effect we are going to see from them in the long run, which will be plagued with volatility.

I’m going to wait a bit longer before deciding anything regarding new additions to my portfolio. A couple of more quarters should be a good indication of where the economy is headed and how the stock market reacts to further tariff implementation. Maybe 6 months will be enough for us to see how these tariffs will affect the overall economy, but I wouldn’t be surprised if we are still talking about it at the end of the summer.

data.title: The Sound Of Silence: What Powell And The Fed Aren't Saying
data.description:
The Sounds Of Silence

Anyone engaged in finance and investing at any level knows what the Fed is and who its principals are. After that, there is little consensus on the Fed, what it is doing, and how it is progressing- even over what its true goal is.

This may appear to be an extreme statement about the Federal Reserve, but despite its eight annual meetings, four congressional testimonies by the chairman each year, post-meeting press conferences with policy statements, numerous speeches by FOMC members, and quarterly releases of quasi-forecasts for the economy and its own policy expectations, there is still little clarity on Fed policy and even less understanding of the reasons behind the choices the Fed has made.

Fed Watching Is A Learning Experience

I began on Wall Street in 1977, working for Paul Volcker at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. I left the Fed in 1983 to be a dedicated Fed-Watcher. So, I have been doing this for a while. I am familiar with what the Fed does today, what it has done in the past, and how the Fed has evolved. Despite different chairs, different members, different challenges and changing ways of operating, much about the Fed remains the same. In the early days when Volcker was the Fed Chair (after he left the NY Fed), there was little in the way of Fed commentary made to the public. There were no announcements on Fed policy after the meetings. The Fed did not divulge its Fed funds target. We were left to watch the statistics that the Fed issues and to make judgments from that on policy. There were still the four Fed chair testimonies each year, at the time, they were called the Humphrey-Hawkins testimonies, since they were required by legislation under that name.

In the early days, we could tell what the Fed was doing by watching the bank reserves market, the Fed’s open market operations, and by knowing that the Fed was ‘targeting money supply growth’ - deriving a path for non-borrowed reserves from the Fed’s target for money growth. When money supply overshot the mark, bank demand for reserves would rise, and those reserves were injected through the discount window. As bank borrowing demand through the Fed’s discount window rose, rates would rise, and we could see what the Fed was doing and make educated guesses at the Fed Funds Rate likely path. Those days are gone.

The Modern Fed Talks To Us... But Tells Us What?

Those days and procedures are replaced by a Fed that tells us what the Fed funds target is, but unlike in the past, when there were monetary targets, we are unclear what will trigger the Fed to change interest rates next. And, yes, I am dead serious about that. For all the talking and statements providing ‘guidance’ and press conferences that the Fed does, it does not speak to us very clearly about what it is doing or even why it is doing it.

The Post-FOMC Press Conference

Chair Powell leads off every single post-Fed meeting press conference with a statement that tells us absolutely nothing. He says the Fed is conducting policy in accordance with its dual mandate. And…? Well, the follow-up to that is to tell us what the Fed did at the meeting and some statement about why – a purposely vague statement.

It’s Not What They Do Say… It's What They Don’t Say

I recommend an approach to Fed watching that delves into understanding what the Fed is choosing not to do and not to say. I realize that is really too broad a directive to be useful. But let me explain the context for that remark. When the Fed chooses not to do, or not say something, the central bank would normally mention it is worth thinking about why.

In A Good Place… What Does This Really Mean?

Currently, the Fed’s most consistent statement is that the Fed is IN A GOOD PLACE. Chair Powell follows this up by saying the Fed can be patient… and he elaborates that…it can either hold rates where they are for longer or cut rates when it is appropriate. What is missing here? Well, what’s missing is the Chair saying that the Fed can either hike rates, keep them unchanged or raise them. The Fed has refused to say ‘it could raise rates’ - even as a possibility- since it has allowed rates to peak. This is an extremely important communication by omission. It is a silent communication. By not saying that at each meeting, Chair Powell is clearly communicating that rate hikes are not even in consideration. The Fed is sensitized to saying it won’t raise rates since it issued such a directive when the Framework agreement was new and its pledge to meet certain conditions before it would raise rates got it in a world of trouble. So now that ‘pledge’ has gone silent. We can ask what kind of ‘place’ is this that is ’so good’ that the Fed will not even consider a rate hike? Probably not a very good place. Probably somewhat tenuous. If the Fed can’t mention rate hikes, something is very wrong.

What Is Lurking In The Shadows, That The Fed Won’t Mention?

Banks have experienced a lot of pressure from the Fed’s policies. The Fed delayed far too long in hiking rates after inflation rose- it waited a year after inflation rose above its target to hike rates. Currently, the Fed cites ‘several studies’ that claim to find that an earlier Fed move would have had little impact on inflation. Can that be true? Contrarily, I have been warning about inflation for some time. In September 2024, I wrote on Seeking Alpha (here) that the Fed was ignoring inflation risk. In late October, I wrote (here) that the Fed’s soft landing objective was getting in the way of making good policy. The Fed’s emphasis on unemployment has become ‘the reason in the spotlight’ for the Fed NOT hiking rates, even though the unemployment rate already is below the level most economists think represents sustainably low unemployment that will not accelerate inflation.

How Could The Timing Of Rate Hikes Not Matter?

First of all, I am very skeptical of research saying quicker action on rate hikes would not have had an impact (a substantial impact) on inflation. The main channel for having an impact would have been through expectations. By not raising rates, the Fed was telling people it was not going to even TRY to resist rising prices. The Fed’s excuses ranged from inflation is not real, to it not lasting, to oooh-boy we’re wrong! But the Fed did not get to that view (#3) for a while, since it had pledged not to hike rates until the economy was back to maximum employment. So, in public communications, the Fed declared various things about the economy that would justify it doing nothing, since that is what it had promised to do as long as the economy was short of ‘maximum employment. One Fed expert cited preliminarily weak job numbers at the end of 2021 for keeping Fed rate hikes at bay as inflation escalated. And then the Fed cited a ‘taper’ it had in place. The ‘taper’ referred to a program of securities purchases (stimulative reserve provision) that was in progress and was being reduced with an eye to stopping it altogether. It’s important to know that the Fed was in complete control of the taper schedule and despite the high inflation it left the taper schedule in place but called it an impediment to hiking rates! Really - you cannot make this up. The Fed argued it could not be buying securities (easing) and raising rates (tightening) at the same time. But then in this cycle, when it decided it had to ease it wanted to keep on shrinking the balance sheet, so suddenly it was just fine to ease (cut Fed funds) and tighten (shrink the balance sheet) at the same time. The Fed does not communicate to us honestly or fairly, and this is the most recent - most stark example I can think of. And it is important to know this about Fed communication.

The Fed Recognized Two Simultaneous Problems But Can Address Only One

When the Fed decided to raise their rate sharply, Jim Bullard (St Louis Fed President) became the chief spokesman for extreme and rapid Fed rate hikes. This action was opposed by Esther George (K.C. Fed President) who argued that such rapid rate hikes would be hard on the market, on banks, on investors and on households. The Fed was lucky to have two such wise members because they were both right- but the Fed could only do one of those things- it chose to raise rates -fast. But Esther’s warning was right, and there were banking problems that followed.

Esther’s Warning Plays Out Again And Again

Not only did banking problems follow, but they lingered. And they HAVE lingered. The problem is that inflation spiked up, and interest rate spiked up and while inflation is down sharply from its peak, it is still higher than before COVID and running at a pace above the target for the PCE (inflation is still above 2%) and PCE inflation has been excessive for 48 months in a row. How can the Fed talk about being patient - meaning it is patiently waiting, planning, to cut rates - when inflation has been above target for 48 months in a row and is still excessive? The unemployment rate is still quite low, above its recent minimum, but low, so what is going on?

The chart above is illustrative of some of the prevailing financial conditions in the economy, particularly as faced by banks, using the 5-year note as an example. It is evidence that bank portfolios are- and have been - under pressure. The only relief is in sight is to let the Fed funds rate fall. (see discussion below)

First, Recognize The Dissonance, Then Decode It

We decode the Fed’s actions by looking at the facts and at what the Fed has not been addressing. In economics, we call this ‘revealed preference’ <<look at what I do.>> The Fed is undeterred about missing and continuing to miss its inflation target. Yet, it is sensitive about potentially disturbing the employment market, which shows all the trapping of being fine. This is asymmetric. The Fed is using its reference to the unemployment rate as a reason to not hike rates and to block rate hikes, to explain why it has not opted to control inflation sooner. It's as though the Fed thinks it is in a very slow, long glide to a soft landing. But, in the meantime, inflation still runs over target and now inflation does not appear to be falling anymore… so when does hitting the 2% target matter? Or does it not matter as long as the Fed declares fealty to the 2% target…in the abstract? And why would the Fed treat its inflation target so shabbily, especially with the unemployment situation still in such good shape? Dissonance galore…

Enter The Fed Decoder Ring (SSHHhhhh - Banking Sector Pressures…)

What is really going on is that this long period of excess inflation has caused interest rates to rise and rise relative to historic standards. The chart above presents statistics for the five-year note. The five-year average of five-year note yields, the Fed Funds target and the year-over-year CPI pace. A quick glance shows inflation above its pre-COVID level. But the five-year current yield is substantially higher than its pre-COVID level. The five-year average yield has been rising above this Pre-COVID level – but critically, the yield on the current five-year note is above the average yield for the past five years. And until just this month, the Fed funds rate has been above the yield on the current five-year note as well as above the five-year average. All this suggests a lot of trouble for securities holders.

Understanding Bank Balance Sheet Pressures

These statistics allow us to think about a five-year note as an asset on bank balance sheets and how it's pricing, as well as bank holdings of past five-year notes - interacts with monetary policy. The banking sector problems developed when the Fed began to hike rates rapidly, the best known of these problems was Silicon Valley Bank, but there were others and even an international repercussion as Credit Swiss failed. The chart above is marked <annotated> to show regions of flow profitability, periods when the Five-year Note yield was above the Fed funds rate (a proxy for its financing costs) and second view of profitability (or not), the average yield on the five-year note over five years relative to the Fed funds rate. Again, fed funds are a proxy for funding costs, while the five-year average is a proxy for the average yield on all five-year notes purchased over the last five years. We also note periods when the current five-year note yield is higher than the five-year average. Those are periods when banks would be dealing with mark-to-market losses on their five-year (original issue) holdings, since the yield of the security when purchased would be on average below the current market yield). Since early 2023 bank five-year note holdings have been under markdown pressure as well as funding pressures where both the current five-year note and the average portfolio would be funded at a loss. These two events create negative cash flows and markdown pressure adds to distress, and they have been in play jointly since late 2022. SVB collapsed in March 2023. At that point, the funding costs were rising the most sharply, since then, they have lingered at high levels to continue to pressure portfolio performance at banks. One option for banks to escape the mark-down pain would be to declare securities HTM- Hold To Maturity. But they would still have to disclose mark-to-market shortfalls in financial footnotes, but they would not need to be reflected losses in P&L statements.

The LAST THING IN THE WORLD that the Fed wants to talk about is a banking problem. So, the Fed does not say it can’t raise rates because it could harm banks that are having issues at current rates. Inflation is not just too high and above target, it is too high, and markets are putting higher yields on securities that are painful to banks. So instead of saying bank pain is too great for the Fed to hike the Fed funds rate, the Fed refers to the rate of unemployment as the reason. But that is no longer a reason. The Fed talks about the labor market. But bank portfolios are under pressure based on this five-year note example. And the same is true using other securities yields…Table below.

Not Just A Problem For The Five-Year Note - The Problem Is Broad

And this funding and mark-to-market problem is not confined to the five-year note. The 2-year note, 3-year, 5-year, and 10-year all have periods when the fed funds target rate is higher than the note yield. In the last 27 months, 24 to 27 of those months have produced the adverse funding cost circumstances for these securities. In the case of the portfolio effect, we look at each cash security compared to an average for its tenor which is a 2-Year average for the 2-year note, a 3-year average for the 3-year note and so on. Viewed that way, 32 to 37 of the past 39 months have seen markdown pressures on portfolios with the cash yield above the average yield for the tenor of the respective security for 2-year, 3-year, 5-year and 10-year notes. The table below presents data including mortgage data divided into 3- simple periods.

The data clearly point to pressure on banks through adverse cash flow effects and portfolio or mark-to-market effects.

Déjà Vu All Over Again… As Yogi Might Say…

I started this report talking about my start on Wall Street. It was at that time that US monetary policy through the S&L industry into wild chaos. So we have seen this before! In the late 1970s, it was the removal of the interest rate ceiling that allowed banks to raise money as inflation and interest rates rose. But, bank (S&L) portfolios were stuffed with mortgages acquired when there were rate ceilings. So, allowing banks to pay market rates to acquire deposits and make loans seems to be the release they needed to lend again. But the ‘unintended consequence’ was the damage higher rates would do to their existing portfolios, as the illustration above shows. Everything on the balance sheet for S&Ls would go to a loss position, and that wiped out the bank’s capital base. The same thing is happening now for a different reason, of course...(not the removal of interest rate ceilings) but also because of the SAME underlying REASON: inflation that got too high too fast. And, of course, the tag-along problem that interest rates then got too high too fast, too. In short, that’s: Too, Too, Too.

The REAL Enemy

Inflation is the enemy, not high interest rates and not rising rates of unemployment. Unemployment rates and interest rates will fluctuate. But when inflation rises it must be ‘corralled’ and to do that rates will be pushed up and that will disrupt the economy and send unemployment higher as well. Stopping rate hikes to preserve low unemployment rates is illogical since that would only cause inflation to take hold and lead to even worse, inflation, higher interest rates, and worse unemployment outcomes in the future. Is that where we are?

The Fed’s Real Policy Is Often Hidden From View

The Fed is not really trying to keep unemployment down, a rate that already is low. It is protecting banks that have been under a lot of pressure. Banks have been afforded a protective mechanism that banks did not have during the late 1970s and early 1980s, the HTM accounting device that allows them to designate securities as being ‘held to maturity’ and, with that, no longer required to mark them to market. Banks also can hedge the securities holdings, but the Fed’s current refusal to admit it might raise rates, probably has banks thinking that hedging is mostly wasted money since the Funds rate will NOT go up. If I am right on this and if inflation does run up on tariffs or because of some other factor, and, if the Fed has to begin to discuss rate hikes again, we could see real tremors in markets as institutions all flock to cover exposed positions at the same time.

Fed-Watching Is Not Just Fed-Listening, You Need To Decode As Well

What I propose is something all real Fed watchers know. You must listen to the Fed very carefully and distill what is being said. When I left the NY Fed in 1983, a colleague of mine said this to me, “We would never lie to you, but we might not tell you the whole truth.” There is a reason regional banks got in more trouble than money center banks when the Fed decided inflation was no longer transitory. Money-center banks and their legions of economists know how to listen to the Fed. The regional banks heard what the Fed said once, then stopped listening because they liked what they heard. That’s never a good idea. With the Fed, the main message is often hidden between the lines. The Fed is always ready to ‘recontract’ on its promises – at least on some of them. One of the decoder keys is to figure out which promise that the Fed has made will trump the others. Read the FOMC meeting ‘minutes.’ Look at the adjectives used ‘some’ members think… ‘most’ members think, … ‘several’ members think… ‘a few’ members think… ‘a couple’ members think…and so on. These descriptions are meant to convey a sense of how, broadly, members hold various views. But the Fed also fills us up with some less useful information. Four times a year, we hear people describe ‘the dots’… the data points representing the view of unnamed Fed member views on future levels of Fed funds. But since we do not know who votes (which of the dots is a voter), we do not know how to weigh and balance shifts in the dots when they occur. Did voters change their minds or non-voters? Surprise! Was all the shifting done by FOMC non-voters? We will never know, and yet people will make a big deal about the changes in the dots.

Unrepentant

And when the Fed makes mistakes, it is loath to admit them. Even now, the Fed is citing studies of how their delayed rate hike did not cost us much. Yet, we know that is wrong, It cost us a banking crisis because after doing nothing for a year it had to raise rates so fast to catch up that it caught banks and others flat-footed.

Mum’s The Word…

For now, the Fed is trying to avoid talking about Administration policy. That’s another red flag, It suggests strongly to me that the Fed is highly critical of what is being done and does not want to make that known. The Fed’s silence is glaringly loud on that point. If the Fed were fine with Administration policies, why would it refuse to talk? Silence is information. Sometimes silence from the Fed will speak loudly to you. It suggests to me also that the Fed is wary of the potential repercussions from Administration policy as well, not just a disagreement. So, listen carefully. We know that just because the Fed is not talking about something does not mean it is not paying attention to it. Talk is cheap, but failing to hear the sounds of silence could be expensive.

data.title: Arcus Biosciences: A High-Risk Speculation With Multiple Large, Near-Term Catalysts
data.description:
Arcus Biosciences (NYSE:RCUS) has undergone a period of mixed developments since last year, and the stock has continued to decline into April 2025. While the company still has sufficient cash and a broad pipeline, the immediate road ahead could be make or break as some of its clinical programs approach major benchmarks.

For now, we see Arcus as an interesting high-risk speculation with several massive upcoming catalysts. We are actively considering accumulating shares as the stock continues to falter.

Gilead's ARC-10 Exit

We cannot discuss a potential investment in Arcus without first touching on Gilead's (GILD) departure from ARC-10. Arcus was collaborating with Gilead Sciences on domvanalimab, which is Arcus' lead TIGIT drug. In January, Gilead decided to stop enrollment in the ARC-10 trial, which was testing the drug in PD-L1-high non-small cell lung cancer. Arcus said that it and Gilead will shift to other studies investigating domvanalimab, such as STAR-121 and STAR-221.

In our view, Gilead stepping away from ARC-10 is not a good sign. Domvanalimab had been positioned as a possible rival to Keytruda. If Gilead had believed that was still the case, it likely would have continued with the trial. That said, the partnership between Gilead and Arcus remains strong, in our view. Gilead holds three seats on Arcus' board, including one held by its Chief Commercial Officer. That gives Gilead a direct say in how Arcus runs its programs. Gilead also recently added $320 million to its stake and now owns roughly 33 percent of the company. In our opinion, that kind of investment shows that Gilead still sees great potential in Arcus.

Why It Matters

We believe Gilead’s move on ARC-10 has more to do with how hard it is to beat Keytruda than with Arcus falling short. Other companies have failed here too. Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Merck (MRK) both tried TIGIT drugs and did not get the results they wanted (see more about Roche's failure here and Merck's here). We believe this puts more pressure on the remaining Arcus studies.

Now, Arcus and Gilead are focused on other patient groups. In our view, that is a better setup, even if the ARC-10 failure was not what investors wanted.

Pipeline and Competition

Arcus is working on several programs. Quemliclustat, a CD73 drug, is in a Phase 3 trial for first-line pancreatic cancer. The earlier ARC-8 study showed better survival than historical data when it was added to chemotherapy. It was a small study, but enough to move the program along.

Pancreatic cancer represents a challenging indication with limited treatment options. The U.S. market for this indication could grow to more than $5 billion by 2032. The first-line treatment for this indication often entails chemotherapy. Arcus will need to show its drug adds meaningful benefit to those regimens. If it does, we believe quemliclustat could acquire significant market share. But we should note that it would likely compete with assets from companies like Ipsen (OTCPK:IPSEY), which now owns Onivyde, and AstraZeneca (AZN), which is developing combinations that include its PD-L1 inhibitor Imfinzi.

Casdatifan, a HIF-2α drug, is in development for clear cell renal cell carcinoma ((ccRCC)). In a small trial, it showed a response rate above 30 percent in heavily treated patients. That looks better than what Merck’s belzutifan showed in a similar group. Gilead declined to license the drug, but Arcus now controls it fully. If new data is promising, it could become a real asset for Arcus (and, of course, Gilead).

The global market for renal cell carcinoma drugs is forecasted to reach $7.3 billion by 2034. Merck’s belzutifan was approved in 2021 and has a head start. But casdatifan, if the data are promising, could offer another treatment option. Whether it competes well will obviously depend on how the Phase 3 results look.

The two programs target different cancers and are based on separate science. In our opinion, that helps de-risk an investment in Arcus. In other words, Arcus is not depending on one drug candidate for its survival. Still, the competition is strong. Belzutifan is already on the market. Pancreatic cancer has had many failed drugs. Arcus will need strong results in Phase 3 to effectively penetrate this market.

Upcoming Catalysts

If Arcus delivers positive Phase 3 results—particularly in pancreatic or kidney cancer—we believe the upside could be significant. The stock is currently trading near cash levels. In our opinion, success in one of these trials could push shares significantly higher, and potentially even to the $20 range, assuming Gilead remains involved, and the data adequately support regulatory filings. So, we believe shareholders will be rewarded if things go as planned.

Given the structure of the Gilead partnership and its history of acquisitions in oncology, we believe a buyout could be possible if one of Arcus’s late-stage programs hits. After all, Gilead owns a significant stake, sits on Arcus’s board, and already has co-development rights to several programs. If Arcus delivers success in one of the ongoing trials, we think an acquisition would at least be on the table. Gilead’s acquisition of Immunomedics in 2020 for $21 billion shows it is willing to pay up when the science justifies it. That said, Gilead passed on casdatifan, which shows it is selective.

We expect updates from STAR-121 (domvanalimab and zimberelimab in NSCLC) and STAR-221 (domvanalimab and zimberelimab in gastric and esophageal cancers) later this year. Quemliclustat's Phase 3 trial may provide an interim update by late 2025 or early 2026. Casdatifan's next set of data is anticipated in late 2025.

Cash and Capital Pressure

Arcus reported $992 million in cash and investments at the end of 2024. That includes Gilead’s equity purchase and a $150 million raise from January. That gives Arcus a significant amount of time to continue with R&D. Arcus spent over $450 million in 2023. In Q4 2024, Arcus reported $111 million in R&D expenses. If spending stays close to that level and if timelines slip, we believe the company may need to raise cash again before 2027.

Our Take

As the stock continues to drop, we are actively considering a high-risk speculation in Arcus. Arcus has multiple shots on goal. If the company surprises Wall Street with positive results from one or more of its Phase 3 clinical trials - which are expected sometime in the second half of 2025 - we believe the stock will rise quickly and exponentially. That said, if the data are underwhelming, we would expect the stock to significantly decline.

Right now, we are comforted by the fact that Arcus has a solid cash position, and Gilead is very much involved and has even recently increased its investment in Arcus. This de-risks our potential speculation in Arcus (though it still remains high-risk).

data.title: GSK: Under Threat From 'Double Whammy' Of Vaccine Skepticism And Pharma Tariffs
data.description:
Investment Overview

These are turbulent times for the "Big Pharma" industry, but it may be ex-US Pharmaceutical companies that are set to suffer the most.

GSK plc (NYSE:GSK), the Middlesex, United Kingdom-headquartered Pharma is a case in point. In my last note on the company, published last December, I suggested the company was "doing all the right things but can't catch a break," and downgraded my "Buy" rating to a "Hold."

Stock is up ~9% since. The company reported a decent set of full year 2024 earnings - as CEO Dame Emma Walmsley put it on the Q4 2024 earnings call:

Our 2024 performance demonstrates the transformation of the business. Sales grew 8% to over £31 billion, with strong growth and increasing contribution from Specialty medicines more than offsetting headwinds in vaccines. Core operating profit was up 13% and core EPS was up 12%. This level of performance delivered two upgrades to guidance in 2024 and supports the increased dividend of 61p per share announced today.

As we can see below, however, on a GAAP basis, operating income was down substantially year-on-year, to $7.74bn, while GSK's top line has shrunk thanks to the divesture its consumer health business into a new entity, Haleon (HLN), which began trading in July 2022.

GSK's strengths today are its HIV business, which generated >$7bn of revenues in 2024, with 4 "Blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) products, a growing oncology business which generated >$1.4bn revenues last year, thanks to recently approved meds Jemperli and Ojjara, indicated for solid tumor cancers and myelofibrosis respectively, a >$3bn per annum immuno / inflammation franchise, a respiratory division worth >$7.2bn per annum, and most importantly, a vaccines division that generated >$9bn in revenues last year.

As we can see above, GSK expects to drive low-to-mid single-digit percentage growth in 2025, and expects to hit £40bn - ~$48bn - revenues by 2031. Core operating margin is expected to be >31% by 2026, and vaccine revenues are set to grow at a CAGR in the "mid to high single digits.

If GSK pulls this off and is close to being a >$50bn revenues per annum Pharma with a >30% core operating margin, then almost inevitably, its share price and market cap valuation - currently ~$77bn, would rise in response to such progress - I'd speculate to well over $100bn, meaning in just a few years, investors could secure a >20% return on their investment.

Trump Administration's Potential Vaccine Skepticism Threaten To Undermine GSK's Vaccine Division Ambitions

When the newly elected Trump administration put forward Robert F Kennedy Jr - widely believed to be a vaccine sceptic based on comments he has made in the past, as the next head of the Department for Health and Human Services ("HHS"), vaccine makers were put on red alert, and recent developments seem to be confirming these companies' worst fears.

Last week saw the resignation of Peter Marks from his role as director of the FDA’s Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER), citing an "unprecedented assault on scientific truth" by Kennedy, although the Wall Street Journal has suggested Marks's role would have been terminated had he not resigned. Apparently, the new HHS administration is planning to cut as many as 10k full-time employees.

Prior to this, the new administration cancelled a meeting to discuss the new COVID strain that vaccine makers should target in 2025, and cancelled a meeting of its Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee ("VRBPAC").

These are not promising signs, although we should note that we don't yet know precisely what stance the HHS and FDA, under its new leader, Marty Makary - not known to bee a vaccine skeptic - will take.

GSK markets and sells two meningitis vaccines, which earned >$1.4bn of revenues in 2024; an influenza vaccine, Fluarix, which earned >$400m revenues; a shingles vaccine which earned >$3.3bn; an RSV vaccine which earned nearly $600m; and several others besides, as part of an "established vaccines" segment which earned >$3.3bn last year.

Naturally, not all all of GSK's vaccine revenue is earned in the US, but a substantial portion is - at a conference in November, CEO Walmsley was quoted as saying:

The US is extremely important to GSK. We’re a global company, but more than half of the business is in the US, we’re very heavily invested there, with factories in many states. It is by far the most important market to bring our innovation to first.

As such, the advent of a vaccine skeptic HHS / FDA ought to be considered a significant problem for GSK. For example, during a recent "fireside chat" at the TD Cowen Healthcare Conference, GSK's Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") Luke Miels told the audience, regarding the ~70m 50-plus US population:

We have 50 million of them already vaccinated with at least one shot of Shingrix. So there's another 22 steady state that we have a good chance of picking up.

In the past, the penetration rate for Shingrix, we added about 6% or 7% a year. So we finished last year at around 40%, 41% penetration. But what we've said consistently is we expect that accrual rate to drop to a rate of 3% to 5%, because you've got less engaged people, less motivated. But I do think we can work through that population. So growth is more challenging, but we can still ultimately capture those patients and vaccinate them over time.

GSK will doubtless be well aware of the challenges ahead, and preparing for them in earnest, but I would speculate there is a good chance the vaccine division may shrink, not grow, over the next several years, as US seniors listen to their government and opt against shots for diseases such as shingles, RSV, COVID, and even influenza.

Tariffs On Pharma Imports May Create Further Problems

There has been intense discussion across the pharmaceutical media around what kind of tariffs the Trump administration may impose after the President confirmed last week he was determined to introduce them.

While Pharma's have been generally reluctant to speculate about what the tariffs may look like, a figure between 20-30% seems likely, which companies are hoping will be phased in over time, and not introduced immediately.

An announcement from the Trump administration had been expected to today, which has been dubbed "Liberation Day," but has not yet come at the time of writing, however tariffs feel more or less inevitable at this point. The tariffs would affect all Pharma and biotech companies, both inside and outside the US, as almost every US pharma company brings in products from abroad, and most ex-US companies are importing their drugs into the US as opposed to manufacturing them there.

Interestingly, GSK CEO Walmsley has said the company will have "no exposure or minimal exposure" to any proposed tariffs. However GSK's stock price is down >2% in trading this week, and it is difficult to see how, despite the fact that GSK does have a significant manufacturing presence within the US, GSK's bottom line would not be significantly impacted by the introduction of tariffs.

The idea behind the imposition of tariffs is to encourage Pharma's to do more of their manufacturing inside the US, which would stimulate the US economy and create jobs, but Pharma's have expressed their opinion that drug shortages may occur in the short-term, which could put patients in danger.

What is clear is that we are entering uncharted territory, and despite intense speculation, nobody can truly say how this scenario will play out.

Concluding Thoughts: Is There Any Method To The Madness - Or Should Expectations For Pharma's Performance Be Downgraded

It's important to make the point that the imposition of tariffs by a Trump Administration, if it were elected, is likely a scenario that Pharma's have been considering for some time, and there will doubtless be strategies in place to cope with what is taking place, and the same goes for vaccines. That does not mean there will no short-term pain, felt, however.

Walmsley has spoken about shifting GSK's focus towards oncology, inflammation and immunology. However such a shift in emphasis cannot be undertaken overnight, and these fields are massive strengths of rival Pharmas, for example, Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) in oncology, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in inflammation, and AbbVie (ABBV) in immunology.

In my previous note, I spoke about GSK "doing the right things but not catching a break," but as things have played out, I am beginning to wonder if GSK was "doing the right things" by, for example, focusing its energies on a new RSV vaccine, Arexvy, when R&D funding may have been directed elsewhere

That's a harsh criticism - Arexvy generated nearly $600m of revenues in 2024, and we still don't know the extent to which a Kennedy-run HHS will interfere with current regulations, or how the US public may react to redirection on vaccine use.

What's clear is that this week, we find ourselves "in the eye of the storm," and Pharma valuations are suffering, but the key question to answer is, are the market's fears overblown? If they are, then theoretically, it's time to accumulate GSK stock, believing in Walmsley's claim that tariffs will have a negligible effect on the business, which seems unlikely, but that's what creates the contrarian trade.

GSK's ADR stock is trading ~$37 at the time of writing, up >10% year-to-date, and about average in terms of five-year share price performance - GSK stock hit a low of $29 after the Haleon (HLN) spinout, and traded as high as $44 in August last year.

At the present time, I don't believe there is enough data available re tariffs or actions on vaccines to make a knee-jerk decision on GSK stock, but I'd speculate we will see further declines before we see any upside, so I would not call a "buy the dip" moment today, and we should also acknowledge that the the Trump administration is "playing with fire" to an extent, and to date, the imposition of tariffs on China, Mexico, and Canada have led to stock market declines for most sectors.

The furor has not yet peaked, and my gut feeling is that GSK stock is at risk of dropping <$30 in 2025 as, despite the CEO's claims, it does appear to have heavy exposure to both vaccines and tariffs on imports to the US.

Amid all the uncertainty, 2025 is not shaping up to be a promising year for GSK, or indeed any pharma company, big or small, but the Trump administration will be aware the world is watching its activities, and that a struggling stock market is a major turn-off for voters, so, like the Pharmas themselves it will doubtless have a longer-term plan, but is reluctant to show its hand at the present time.

As such, if you are contemplating buying GSK stock today, I would advise "hold your fire" in 2025, as while volatility feels inevitable, the situation will take not stabilize for some time yet. If GSK stock does fall >$30, it would be tempting to buy, as the Trump administration is unlikely to want to be burdened with a failing Pharma sector, and can pledge to produce more in the US as the likes of Eli Lilly (LLY) and AbbVie have already done.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Waton Financial: Complex Structure Poses Downside Risk
data.description:
While the Newsmax (NMAX) IPO has grabbed all the headlines, shares of Waton Financial Limited (NASDAQ:WTF) have also been incredibly volatile since their IPO this week. After pricing at $4, the stock surged towards $20, only to fall 60% to about $8. This is still twice its initial offering. With such wild trading, WTF is worth exploring to determine if there is a significant dislocation from its underlying fair value. I am bearish.

Waton Financial's small float will keep shares volatile

As we have now seen several times post-COVID, when the available float of shares is small and a stock gains momentum, it can trade to extreme levels beyond expectations. We saw this with GameStop (GME), AMC Entertainment (AMC), Avis Budget (CAR), and now more recently NMAX. I believe WTF also falls into this category, as its IPO was quite small, issuing just 4.4 million shares to raise about $17.5 million. It subsequently exercised the entire over-allotment option, bringing the number of shares issued to about 5 million.

After using this over-allotment, Waton has about 48.2 million shares, so only about 10% of the company was issued to the public. As part of this offering, Waton, its directors, and owners of 5+% of shares have all agreed not to sell stock for up to six months. In situations like this, when the initial float is small and large investors are locked up, shares can be incredibly volatile. While Waton has a ~$400 million market capitalization, only about $40 million of stock is initially freely trading. As such, relatively small trades can cause seemingly large swings in the market cap and share prices. This is exactly what happened on the day of the IPO.

Zhou Kai is the Chairman of the Board. He owns 86% of the company after the offering, and as a consequence, the float is likely to remain restricted for some time. In the long term, he may have brought the company public as a way to enhance his liquidity and monetize some of his stake. In the near term, given lock-ups, share sales will not occur. Because the float is so small, and Waton is a controlled company, it is likely going to be difficult for shares to sustain much of a following in the investment community, which can weigh on liquidity, leaving WTF prone to large swings.

Corporate governance is a risk

This will be further complicated by its byzantine corporate structure with over a dozen different entities, despite being rather small with about $10 million in revenue in its prior fiscal year. Within its web of entities, Waton has historically operated as a controlled company. For instance, it invested in Micro Connect, which offered daily revenue contracts, only to sell its stake to its Chair Zhou Kai, a related party, for no gain or loss.

Now prior to the IPO, Zhou Kai owned ~95% of the company, so frankly, whether he over or under-paid for the stake was not all that relevant financially, since the company was essentially all his. From a corporate governance perspective, selling investments at cost to management is dubious and filled with conflicts of interest. For WTF to be a credible public company, it will need to operate like one, putting firewalls between corporate and personal activities to ensure that value is not being extracted from the company to the benefit of its Chairman.

These conflicts run deeper and are embedded within its operations. A key growth focus of the company is selling software licenses to its broker customers to enhance their digital trading. However, it is dependent on another entity, Shenzhen Jinhui, to sell these licenses. That company is controlled by its Chairman. This creates a conflict in terms of pricing the contracts, and it creates an incentive to keep using this entity even if its offering has been surpassed.

Its business is also dependent on Wealth Guardian Investment (WGI), given shared management. And in past years and quarters, 40% to 80% of its revenue has come from trading by this entity. If it ever lost WGI as a customer, Waton would be seriously impaired, and I would like to see the company better diversify its revenue base. I would also note that WSI has extended a $4.3 million credit line to WGI to support margin trading, further entangling it with its key customer. There is also the risk that Waton provides material support to WGI, should WGI have trouble, in order to maintain trading revenues. Considering all of these risks, Waton’s ownership structure and related party mix are significant red flags when considering an investment.

Revenue is volatile and may not return to fiscal 2024 levels

In terms of its own fundamentals, Waton provides technology and trading services to small- and medium-sized brokers. Its brokerage and commission income is dependent on trading volume. It also provides access to IPO and new bond deals. Waton primarily operates in Hong Kong, even as it is a British Virgin Islands holding company. It does not materially operate in mainland China, but if China decides to exert more control over Hong Kong, that would be a risk.

Its business will be closely tied to trading volumes and sentiment in Hong Kong. As you can see, the Hong Kong stock market has been a significant long-term underperformer, though it recently has enjoyed an uptick, which should be a benefit to Waton’s results.

In its fiscal 2024 (which ends in March), revenue nearly doubled to $10 million from $4.7 million. However, in the six months ending September 20th, revenue growth was under 30% to $3.0 million. 68% of revenue year to date comes from its related parties. As you can see, brokerage and commission income is the primary source of revenue. The pattern here is notable. In fiscal 2024, revenue in this line item was $6.5 million, but in the past six months, it was under $400k. For a business that does not believe it has seasonality, this is a jarring drop.

Frankly, it appears the 2024 numbers may have been a one-time strong spot in my view. Looking at a quarterly cadence, the end of 2023 spiked to $4.4 million, more than every other quarter put together. In fiscal 2024, it generated $5.8 million in bond distribution fees. The level of income from bond distribution fees does not appear replicable to me, and I would caution against forecasting a continuation of $10 million in annual revenue. While its software business is growing, this appears to be a ~$6-7 million revenue company.

With the proceeds, Waton aims to change its business mix. Right now, revenue is primarily tied to trading levels, which, as you can see above, are incredibly volatile. Its move into software licensing speaks to a desire to generate more recurring and predictable revenue. With the proceeds, it plans to invest to improve the functionality of its trading platform and develop its asset management business, alongside general corporate purposes. We can already see the company ramping up spending for new business verticals. Operating costs have risen by 35% (outpacing revenue growth) to $3.9 million due to higher compensation costs.

It is planning to launch funds to generate recurring asset management fees and will likely try to sell products to its network of ~600 broker customers. Managing funds is significantly different from offering digitized trading platforms, and whether Waton can achieve success here is unknown, especially as it often takes several years to develop a track record in order to win institutional clients.

Waton also has fairly limited resources. As of September 30th, Waton had $3 million in cash and $3.5 million in investments, with a total shareholders' equity of $9.7 million. This deal provides $5 million of further cash, but Waton is burning about $1 million every six months. At a ~0.5% management fee, in order to generate $20 million in fee revenue, it would need to raise $4 billion. With a ~$350-400 million market cap, this is likely what Waton would need to achieve to justify its valuation. This will be a multiyear effort that may well not succeed.

Given the litany of related party and ownership concerns I have, there is a high hurdle to invest in a stock like Waton, in my view. Frankly, they make the company nearly uninvestable, as I would want to see it operate several quarters as a public company to ensure it is operating with appropriate procedures to manage the clear conflicts of interest it faces. When we combine that with the fact that its strong fiscal 2024 revenue appears to be an anomaly and the questionable capacity to build a large asset management business with its limited capital, I view WTF as a sell.

Given its small float, spikes like we saw on its IPO can occur, but a ~$400 million valuation for a company with under $50 million of book equity is difficult to justify absent a clear path to growth I do not see. Over time, I expect shares to languish and likely return to their IPO level of $4. I would avoid shares and look for opportunities elsewhere in companies with cleaner management structures.
